title: Where Did the Monk‚Äôs Haircut Come From? A Look at the Rich and Contentious History of the Tonsure
description:
in History, Religion |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

One might assume from a mod¬≠ern view¬≠point that the hair¬≠styles worn by monks a
body:
in History, Religion |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

One might assume from a mod¬≠ern view¬≠point that the hair¬≠styles worn by monks arose to deal with male pat¬≠tern bald¬≠ness anx¬≠i¬≠ety. As in the school uni¬≠form approach, you can‚Äôt sin¬≠gle out one person‚Äôs bald¬≠ness when every¬≠one is bald. But this, again, would be a mod¬≠ern view, full of the van¬≠i¬≠ty the tonsured‚Äîthose with reli¬≠gious¬≠ly shaven heads‚Äîostensibly vowed to renounce. Accord¬≠ing to the Catholic Ency¬≠clo¬≠pe¬≠dia, the ton¬≠sure (from the Latin verb for ‚Äúto shear‚Äù) began as a ‚Äúbadge of slav¬≠ery‚Äù among Greeks and Romans. It was adopt¬≠ed ‚Äúon this very account‚Äù by ear¬≠ly monas¬≠tic orders, to mark the total sur¬≠ren¬≠der of the will.
Would it sur¬≠prise you, then, to learn that there were ton¬≠sure wars? Prob¬≠a¬≠bly not if you know any¬≠thing about church his¬≠to¬≠ry. Every arti¬≠cle of cloth¬≠ing and of faith has sparked some major con¬≠tro¬≠ver¬≠sy at one time or anoth¬≠er. So too with the ton¬≠sure, of which‚Äîwe learn in the Vox video above‚Äîthere were three styles. The first, the coro¬≠nal (or Roman or Petrine) ton¬≠sure, is the one we see in count¬≠less Medieval and Renais¬≠sance paint¬≠ings: a bald pate at the crown sur¬≠round¬≠ed by a fringe of hair, pos¬≠si¬≠bly meant to evoke the crown of thorns. Next is the Pauline, a ful¬≠ly shaved head, seen much less in West¬≠ern art since it was ‚Äúused more com¬≠mon¬≠ly in East¬≠ern Ortho¬≠doxy.‚Äù
The third style of ton¬≠sure caused all the trou¬≠ble. Or rather, it was this style that served as a vis¬≠i¬≠ble sign of reli¬≠gious dif¬≠fer¬≠ences between the Roman Catholic Church and the church¬≠es in Britain and Ire¬≠land. ‚ÄúCeltic Catholi¬≠cism was ‚Äòout of sync‚Äô with the Roman Catholic Church,‚Äù notes Vox. ‚ÄúRoman Catholics would use the dif¬≠fer¬≠ences between them to por¬≠tray Celtic Catholi¬≠cism as pagan, or even as an off¬≠shoot, cel¬≠e¬≠brat¬≠ing the pow¬≠er-hun¬≠gry magi¬≠cian, Simon Magus.‚Äù The Celtic ton¬≠sure fell under a cloud, but how exact¬≠ly did it dif¬≠fer from the oth¬≠ers? Since it dis¬≠ap¬≠peared in the ear¬≠ly Mid¬≠dle Ages and few images seem to have sur¬≠vived, no one seems sure.
Daniel McCarthy, fel¬≠low emer¬≠i¬≠tus at Trin¬≠i¬≠ty Col¬≠lege, Dublin set out to solve the mys¬≠tery. He spec¬≠u¬≠lates that the Celtic ton¬≠sure, as you‚Äôll see on a com¬≠put¬≠er-sim¬≠u¬≠lat¬≠ed monk‚Äôs head, was a tri¬≠an¬≠gu¬≠lar shape, with the apex at the front. When the Roman Catholics took over Ire¬≠land, all of the vest¬≠ments, dates, and hair¬≠cuts were slow¬≠ly brought into line with the dom¬≠i¬≠nant view. The prac¬≠tice of ton¬≠sure offi¬≠cial¬≠ly end¬≠ed in 1972, and fell out of favor in Eng¬≠lish-speak¬≠ing coun¬≠tries cen¬≠turies ear¬≠li¬≠er, accord¬≠ing to the Catholic Ency¬≠clo¬≠pe¬≠dia. But in any case, McCarthy sees the ton¬≠sure not as a spurn¬≠ing of fash¬≠ion, but as a cult-like devo¬≠tion to style. In that sense, we can see peo¬≠ple who adopt sim¬≠i¬≠lar hair¬≠cuts around the world as still visu¬≠al¬≠ly sig¬≠nal¬≠ing their mem¬≠ber¬≠ship in some kind of order, reli¬≠gious or oth¬≠er¬≠wise.
Note: An ear¬≠li¬≠er ver¬≠sion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
How a Bal¬≠ti¬≠more Hair¬≠dress¬≠er Became a World-Renowned ‚ÄúHair Archae¬≠ol¬≠o¬≠gist‚Äù of Ancient Rome
Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed: Stephen Fry & Ann Wid¬≠de¬≠combe Debate the Catholic Church
50 Years of Chang¬≠ing David Bowie Hair Styles in One Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed GIF
Josh Jones is a writer and musi¬≠cian based in Durham, NC.

title: The World‚Äôs Greatest Song That Simply Shouldn‚Äôt Exist: The Making of The Beatles‚Äô ‚ÄúA Day in the Life‚Äù
description:
in History, Music |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Were we to take bets on the sin¬≠gle most wide¬≠ly heard alarm clock, the one includ
body:
in History, Music |   	July 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Were we to take bets on the sin¬≠gle most wide¬≠ly heard alarm clock, the one includ¬≠ed on the Bea¬≠t¬≠les‚Äô ‚ÄúA Day in the Life‚Äù would sure¬≠ly be a pop¬≠u¬≠lar choice. It comes, you‚Äôll recall, at the begin¬≠ning of the song‚Äôs mid¬≠dle sec¬≠tion, when Paul takes over from John and sings about wak¬≠ing up, comb¬≠ing his hair, catch¬≠ing a bus, and hav¬≠ing a smoke. Giv¬≠en the lyrics‚Äô sub¬≠ject mat¬≠ter, its inclu¬≠sion seems nat¬≠ur¬≠al, and even like one of the most straight¬≠for¬≠ward¬≠ly explic¬≠a¬≠ble choic¬≠es in the entire record¬≠ing. In fact, the alarm clock was nev¬≠er meant to stay in the mix: the band‚Äôs assis¬≠tant Mal Evans set it off as a gag after doing a place¬≠hold¬≠er count of 24 mea¬≠sures in the mid¬≠dle of the ses¬≠sions, but it just hap¬≠pened to work per¬≠fect¬≠ly with the form the song even¬≠tu¬≠al¬≠ly took.
That‚Äôs just one of the for¬≠tu¬≠itous acci¬≠dents music YouTu¬≠ber David Hart¬≠ley recounts in the video above, on the com¬≠plete mak¬≠ing of ‚ÄúA Day in the Life.‚Äù As most Bea¬≠t¬≠les fans know, John real¬≠ly did take his ini¬≠tial inspi¬≠ra¬≠tion from the news¬≠pa¬≠per, and in par¬≠tic¬≠u¬≠lar a cou¬≠ple of arti¬≠cles about a Guin¬≠ness heir‚Äôs fatal car crash and 4,000 holes in Black¬≠burn, Lan¬≠cashire.
But after he wrote his vers¬≠es, he found him¬≠self at a loss as to how to devel¬≠op the song from there. Paul offered the bit he‚Äôd already writ¬≠ten about falling out of bed and drag¬≠ging a comb across his head. It was¬≠n‚Äôt long before the Bea¬≠t¬≠les began record¬≠ing ‚Äî and doing so for the first time, Hart¬≠ley notes, before hav¬≠ing fin¬≠ished writ¬≠ing the song. They had dis¬≠parate pieces of music to join togeth¬≠er, but no idea, yet, of how they‚Äôd do it.
Hence those 24 emp¬≠ty bars. Play¬≠ing a series of ear¬≠ly takes, Hart¬≠ley shows how they got to be filled in, draw¬≠ing heav¬≠i¬≠ly on Here, There, and Every¬≠where: My Life Record¬≠ing the Music of the Bea¬≠t¬≠les, the mem¬≠oir of engi¬≠neer Geoff Emer¬≠ick. John‚Äôs idea of ‚Äúa sound that began real¬≠ly small, and then grad¬≠u¬≠al¬≠ly expand¬≠ed into some¬≠thing huge‚Äù inspired Paul‚Äôs sug¬≠ges¬≠tion of a sym¬≠pho¬≠ny orches¬≠tra, in response to which pro¬≠duc¬≠er George Mar¬≠tin expressed con¬≠cerns about the cost of hir¬≠ing one. Ringo, in turn, jok¬≠ing¬≠ly pro¬≠posed hir¬≠ing half the orches¬≠tra and hav¬≠ing them play the part twice, which turned out to be a prag¬≠mat¬≠ic solu¬≠tion. As for how to end a song like this, the use of ‚Äúmaybe the most famous sin¬≠gle chord in record¬≠ed his¬≠to¬≠ry‚Äù ‚Äî fol¬≠lowed by ‚Äúa high-fre¬≠quen¬≠cy tone only dogs could hear and a short loop of non¬≠sense sound‚Äù ‚Äî¬Ýcame as the result of a process we can only call Beat¬≠lesque.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
How John Lennon Wrote the Bea¬≠t¬≠les‚Äô Best Song, ‚ÄúA Day in the Life‚Äù
Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: A High-Definition Cinematic Video Relives NASA's Artemis II Mission
description: Between April 1 and 10 of this year, NASA‚Äôs Artemis II mission made history as four astronauts flew farther from Earth than anyone has ever travel
body:
Between April 1 and 10 of this year, NASA‚Äôs Artemis II mission made history as four astronauts flew farther from Earth than anyone has ever traveled before. With state-of-the-art technology and numerous cameras onboard, the crew captured some of the most striking images we‚Äôve seen of our planet as the flight path led them around our iconic satellite. Lenses attached to the exterior of the spacecraft, plus numerous photographers stationed on the ground during the launch, documented the literally out-of-the-this world process of rocketing through the planet‚Äôs atmosphere and keeping course around the moon.
John D. Boswell, a musician and filmmaker who works as MELODYSHEEP, created a cinematic short film compiled from videos and images published by NASA. His original music accompanies the public domain images and soundbites, providing a uniquely immersive glimpse of the historic mission. See more of his work on his Vimeo and YouTube channels.

schema.headline: A High-Definition Cinematic Video Relives NASA's Artemis II Mission

title: Presence, Memory, and Time Merge in the Surreal Works of 'Architects of Absence'
description: ‚ÄúNothingness lies coiled in the heart of being,‚Äù philosopher and writer Jean-Paul Sartre posited in Being and Nothingness, published in 1943. It‚Äôs
body:
‚ÄúNothingness lies coiled in the heart of being,‚Äù philosopher and writer Jean-Paul Sartre posited in Being and Nothingness, published in 1943. It‚Äôs in this book that he also shares an anecdote about stepping into a cafe, where he was to meet a friend. In scanning the room with its other patrons, furniture, and noise, he was tuned into the fact that his friend was not there, an experience infused with an acute awareness‚Äîan absence that transformed into a kind of presence.
Architects of Absence, a group exhibition at Gallery Wendi Norris, plumbs this dialogue between two seemingly contradictory elements. Colossal readers may recognize the work of Dabin Ahn, Selva Aparicio, and Remedios Varo (1908-1963) alongside Marcel Jean (1900-1993) and Yaxkin Fuentes-Barrientos. The latter is currently artist-in-residence at the Frank Lloyd Wright-designed Martin House in Buffalo, New York, where she sources disused clay roof tiles on which she then carved fallen leaves.
Dabin Ahn, ‚ÄúNocturne IV‚Äù (2026), oil on linen over panel, jasper, brass, adhesive, camphor, and sapele in walnut artist‚Äôs frame, 13 x 11 inches
Through surreal compositions in a variety of media, including Fuentes-Barrientos‚Äô architectural graphite drawings and oil paintings and Ahn‚Äôs dreamy meditations on loss and the passage of time, viewers are invited to reflect upon the nature of not only presence in a physical sense but also the emotional and psychological sense of being present. Silhouettes, cutouts, buildings and sky, and light and shadow assemble into symbolic motifs of remembrance and visibility.
The gallery says, ‚ÄúWhether investigating the psychic, mythic, and alchemical realms of the imagination, documenting the material remnants of industrial and biological change, or harnessing loss and memory into resonant forms, their works map the absences that define reality.
Architects of Absence continues through August 29 in San Francisco. 
Yaxkin Fuentes-Barrientos, ‚ÄúDormant Pulpits‚Äù (2026), oil and pastel on linen, 24 x 30 inches
Dabin Ahn, ‚ÄúTorso (Full Moon)‚Äù (2026), oil on linen over panel, mother of pearl inlay, cast resin, and hardware, 32 x 12 inches
Selva Aparicio, ‚ÄúRoof Traces II‚Äù (2026), carved clay roof tiles, courtesy of Frank Lloyd Wright‚Äôs Martin House, Buffalo, New York, 12 x 6 x 1/2 inches
Marcel Jean, ‚ÄúConductibilit√© des roches m√®res‚Äù (1974), gouache and flottage on Masonite, 7 1/4 x 8 5/8 inches
Dabin Ahn, ‚ÄúGolden Days (Animated)‚Äù (2026), oil on linen over panel, plexiglass, single-board computer, LCD display with video, cables, power supply, hardware, and walnut, 15 3/4 x 9 1/2 inches
Yaxkin Fuentes-Barrientos, ‚ÄúSpirit of the Beehive‚Äù (2026), ink on found paper, 10 x 13 1/2 inches (framed)

title: Open Call 2027: Jentel Artist Residency
description:
Announcement
                A Mountain West residency for artists, writers, and composers.
body:
Announcement
                A Mountain West residency for artists, writers, and composers. 
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Ann Lovett, October 2011 Jentel resident, works in her studio
    
        
            Located 20 miles southeast of Sheridan, Wyoming, the UW Neltje Center‚Äôs¬ÝJentel Artist Residency, now in its 25th¬Ýyear, offers dedicated individuals a supportive environment to further their artistic development. Jentel offers 3-week residencies from mid-January through early December with private studios and access to Wyoming‚Äôs natural beauty.¬ÝWe welcome visual artists in all mediums; writers in fiction, creative nonfiction, and poetry; and composers in all genres of music. Artistic merit and promise form the basis for selections. Established as well as emerging artists are encouraged to apply.Jentel lies in the Lower Piney Creek Valley with spectacular views over scoria-topped hills to the majestic Bighorn Mountains. Five new and two renovated structures evoke the agricultural and historical setting of the valley and echo the sweeping lines of the foothills and mountains. Accommodations for each resident include a private room, a shared living area and kitchen, and a separate studio space. A $300 stipend helps defray the cost of living and food expenses. Typically, three artists, two writers, and a composer are invited to participate in each of 11 annual 3-week sessions.The application deadline for the Winter/Spring 2027 residencies (January through April) is September 15, 2026.¬ÝApplications for Summer/Fall 2027 residencies (May through November) open on October 1, 2026, and close on January 15, 2027.For more information and to apply, visit jentelarts.org.

title: Trump Whitewashes George Washington‚Äôs Slavery History in New Display
description:
News
                The panels installed by the administration in Philadelphia come just six months after Trump ordered the removal
body:
News
                The panels installed by the administration in Philadelphia come just six months after Trump ordered the removal of the outdoor exhibition Freedom and Slavery in the Making of a New Nation.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            A panel describing "the dirty business of slavery" is no longer included in an exhibition that the Trump administration altered at the Independence National Historical Park. (photo public domain via Save Our Signs)
    
        
            Following a legal battle between the city of Philadelphia and the Trump administration, federal officials replaced exhibits describing the history of slavery in the United States at a Pennsylvania historical park.¬ÝLast Wednesday, July 15, the Trump administration complied with a court order to restore exhibits it had abruptly removed from Independence National Historical Park in January as part of an order to ensure its exhibits did not ‚Äúinappropriately disparage Americans past or living.‚Äù However, federal judges this month allowed the administration to install altered panels that minimize the Founding Fathers' participation in the institution of slavery, according to local activists.¬ÝThe new texts replace the longstanding outdoor exhibition, Freedom and Slavery in the Making of a New Nation, co-created by the city and the federal government at the President‚Äôs House site in 2010 and removed in January under President Trump‚Äôs orders.Michael Coard, a trial attorney and founder of the advocacy group Avenging The Ancestors Coalition (ATAC), which joined the city‚Äôs lawsuit against the Trump administration over the panel removals, described the new exhibits to  Hyperallergic as ‚Äú‚Ääsanitized, whitewashed, and censored.‚ÄùSince 2002, ATAC has led a push to compel the National Park Service to create a permanent memorial dedicated to the nine individuals Washington enslaved.A new panel at the site about the Washington family does not mention slavery. (photo courtesy Michael Coard)Coard said the new panels overemphasized George Washington‚Äôs role in drafting the Fairfax Resolves, a 1774 document that called for the end of the ‚Äúwicked,‚Äù ‚Äúcruel,‚Äù and ‚Äúunnatural‚Äù slave trade. The new texts also make no mention of the hundreds of individuals whom the president enslaved, Coard said.¬Ý‚ÄúPrivately, George Washington often expressed discomfort with the institution and a desire to see it abolished,‚Äù the alternative text reads. ‚ÄúYet as a Virginia plantation owner, his wealth and livelihood were deeply tied to it.‚Äù‚Äú‚ÄäIf he had discomfort, what the hell did they have?‚Äù Coard said. ‚ÄúWhy highlight the discomfort of the victimizer and ignore the hell of the victims?‚ÄùAnother new text reads: ‚ÄúSlaves living in the President‚Äôs House experienced a greater modicum of autonomy than elsewhere in the South, such as to explore the city and sometimes even attend the theater, with Washington buying the tickets.‚Äù‚Äú‚ÄäIt [is] outrageous because the definition of slavery is no autonomy,‚Äù Coard said.¬ÝIn February, Philadelphia federal judge Cynthia M. Rufe ordered the administration to restore the exhibition in a scathing opinion that compared Trump‚Äôs tactics to those of George Orwell‚Äôs fictional Big Brother character. The Trump administration then tried to install its new versions of the plaques, but was prevented by an appeals court from doing so until this month.¬Ý‚Äú‚ÄäYou can try to tidy up by putting out this information about George Washington, but the bottom line is that you don't like the issue of slavery being taught,‚Äù Coard said.In a statement to Hyperallergic, the Department of the Interior (DOI) said the agency was ‚Äúpleased the Courts have sided with the Trump administration on restoring truth and sanity to our country in our nation's 250th.‚Äù‚Äú[The exhibits] acknowledge the evils of slavery, including its injustices and hypocrisies, and, by telling the stories of the nine slaves that Washington kept in the President‚Äôs House, remind us of their essential humanity,‚Äù the DOI spokesperson continued. ‚ÄúThe panels also recall the price our nation paid to finish the work that the Founders had begun and end slavery in the United States once and for all.‚ÄùCoard said that he and ATAC would pursue ‚Äúall available legal options‚Äù in their fight against the federal government for the new exhibition.¬Ý¬Ý‚Äú‚ÄäWhat this president has done with regard to the President's House lays the foundation for himself or the next right-wing fascist president to get rid of any symbols he doesn't like,‚Äù Coard said.
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        Philadelphia
                      
                      
                        Pennsylvania
                      
                      
                        National Park Service
                      
                      
                        Trump Administration

title: The Sunflower Effect of Kyung-Me
description:
Art Review
                The artist‚Äôs current series marks a shift in her practice, evoking the life span of a sunflower ‚Äî fro
body:
Art Review
                The artist‚Äôs current series marks a shift in her practice, evoking the life span of a sunflower ‚Äî from blooming to dying, from sharply defined forms to rivulets of dissolution.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Kyung-Me, "Untitled" (2024), ink and watercolor on mulberry paper (all images courtesy the artist and Bureau, New York)
    
        
            Kyung-Me has yet to be recognized as one of the most compelling artists of her generation. Now in her mid-30s, she has transformed her process to make at least four distinct bodies of work. First, she published her graphic novel, Bad Korean (2016), a pictorial diary done in pastel and colored pencil of a gullible young woman in New York, looking for love. Alienated and unhappy, she believes sex, alcohol, food, and dressing hip will help her escape her hyper-awareness of not fitting in. Told in snippets of speech, such as the repeated phrase ‚ÄúI want to be your only friend,‚Äù the self-deprecating narrative is an engrossing combination of quiet desperation and blunt humor. It is easy to imagine that Kyung-Me could have authored a series of books about this character, but she didn‚Äôt. In Copy Kitty (2020), Kyung-Me used graphite to depict an intricate, wordless tale of a cat who keeps changing her identity in the pursuit of love and affection from a young blonde man.While the themes of alienation and trying to fit in ‚Äî a common trope for those living in the diaspora ‚Äî convey a narrative ripe for exploration, Kyung-Me shifted everything about her work for her first solo exhibition in 2022 at Bureau, which I reviewed. Working meticulously with pen and pencil, she created eight drawings of elaborately detailed, nested rooms. About this dream house, whose occupant‚Äôs face is always hidden, I wrote that it was "as if she drew each line with a razor blade that cuts deep but draws no blood. The effect is mesmerizing and unsettling."Kyung-Me, "Untitled" (2025), ink and watercolor on mulberry paperNone of what I knew of Kyung-Me‚Äôs work prepared me for her current exhibition, I, Sunflower, also at Bureau. Working in ink and watercolor on mulberry paper, she has painted sunflowers that occupy that perceptual place between figural image and abstraction, depiction and the materiality of her medium. After working for a decade drawing women, their faces hidden, in nested rooms, Kyung-Me might have felt she was mimicking her subject and did something unheard of in the art world. She studied a new medium, ink painting, which is the fundamental opposite of everything she had previously done. Instead of adding a new medium to her repertoire, she decided to reinvent herself, forsaking her preoccupation with perfection for improvisation and the loss of control. As the exhibition‚Äôs title conveys, the work is a diary of the day‚Äôs activities, her changing moods and conflicting feelings.Working quickly on different-sized sheets of paper, Kyung-Me evokes a sunflower in different stages ‚Äî from blooming to dying, from sharply defined forms to rivulets of dissolution. Only the ones that meet her satisfaction survive, which, for an artist devoted to perfection, must present a challenge.Installation view of Kyung-Me: I, Sunflower at BureauThe four largest works show a form in the process of liquefaction, a state underscored by the dripping watercolor and ink. Using black and green against a yellow and orange ground, a form made largely of dripping black ink becomes a woman with her head looking toward the earth, long hair flowing down, both dissolving and in grief. Another 2025 ‚ÄúUntitled‚Äù work evokes the sun‚Äôs curved surface, marked by a long vertical black scar and red atmosphere. Multiple kinds of marks are found in a group of similarly sized views of sunflowers seen close-up. Within her circumscribed subject, Kyung-Me pushes her capacity for mark-making in many different directions, celebrating the freedom she gained by changing her process, even as she contemplates evolution and mortality. The title ‚ÄúGrowing, Changing, Regressing, Raging‚Äù (2025) conveys some of the feelings she has poured into the work.Few contemporary artists have undergone the radical changes Kyung-Me has in her work, all while staying true to the desire to evoke autobiography without grounding it in an ‚ÄúI.‚Äù She has pursued her trajectory without regard to stylistic consistency. To do so is to be fearless and vulnerable.Kyung-Me: I, Sunflower continues at Bureau (112 Duane Street, Tribeca, Manhattan) through July 31. The exhibition was organized by the gallery.
                  
                      
                        Art Review
                      
                      
                        Review
                      
                      
                        New York
